19 min read + 44 video
Let’s start with a 10,000′ overview of a PLS:
Product Launch Series (PLS)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 34 --

Visualization of our AoE (Oct 2021) enrollment PLS.
A Product Launch Series is used for time-limited promotions with
a hard open/close. Similar to a Story-Powered Promotion, a PLS is
trigged by active intent.
Together, the PLS and SPP are AoE’s Revenue Engine.
Because a PLS is live and interactive, it’s the perfect feedback loop
in which to engage with an active audience. This live signal allows
a promotion to adapt as it plays out, day after day, towards the
inevitable hard close. Urgency doesn’t need to be emphasized
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 34 --

because it’s always there in the background subtly driving the
action.
For this reason, the PLS is the perfect feedback incubator. What
we learn from a PLS can used to inform and create a SPP Stack.
Once you fully internalize the power of a Bridge Email — pulling
the attention of only the segment of people who are not only
interested, but ready and motivated — the PLS embodies the idea
of elegant simplicity.
Unlike an SPP Stack, the PLS has no fancy automation.
The best way to teach our process, is to show our work and our
thinking. While every PLS is uniquely different, the fundamental
principles are always present.
We’re going to share a lot of emails in this module to demonstrate
multiple PLSs, from small promotions to bigger ones.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 34 --

It’s important to note that these campaign emails aren’t meant to
be ‘deconstructed’ into whatever elements of in�uence you think
we engineered. Don’t do that. You’ll read into things that aren’t
there, or we didn’t think of when writing these emails. Our
process is very organic and will likely differ from yours
signi�cantly.
The emails are meant to give you a glimpse into what we do. Our
creative process. Our thinking before and during writing the
campaigns.
Again, the structure of these emails isn’t meant to be copied or
even modeled if modeling involves trying to analyze what we did
and then try to replicate it as a template. Don’t do that. It won’t
bene�t you for two reasons.
�. Our audience is different from yours. It’s unique to us. We
write to our audience. If we wrote to your audience, we
would communicate differently.
�. The insights of systems theory reveal that to understand the
function of a system (the why something works the way it
does), one has to look at the system it’s a part of, not
reductively deconstruct (analyze) the parts. When
something is deconstructed into its component parts, all
emergent behavior is lost. You can think of ’emergent
behavior’ as the magic that can happen when the synergies of
parts (emails) interact.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 34 --

In this lesson, we’ll do our best to externalize our thinking — why
we wrote what we did. These insights will shine a completely
different light on the emails we share later, informing your own
ideas in a meaningful way.
If you were a part of these promotions, you’d likely have a
memory of the experience from the prospects’ perspective. Now
we’re going to show you this from our vantage point.
Case Study 1: Ideas to Assets (Nov 29 — Dec 2,
2019)
Thesis/theme (controlling idea): Use the creative work we created for our
client to inform our creative framework, document it, share (in our PLS
emails to prospects), and in a masterclass (to customers).
This promotion was very unique for two reasons. It was our �rst
collaboration (for both of us). To try and remember what we had
said before deciding we would even do this, we went back to our
email conversations, some of which we’ll share here. (Some parts
have been redacted for privacy.)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 34 --

We couldn’t �nd where André had pitched the idea to Shawn, but
below was Shawn’s response over email:
This formed the spine of our idea and channeled our thinking.
In most cases, we know what product we are going to promote.
But in some cases, as with this Black Friday, discovering what to
create and pitch was a creative process that emerged.
As you can see from the calendar below, we pitched our idea
within our newsletter �ve days later, then one �nal time on
Monday 25. André landed in New York on Sunday 24. Monday
was our �rst full day collaborating on the work we were doing for
our client.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 34 --

What worked well for us is that at the end of each day, with a
celebratory Vermont craft beer each, we externalized our ideas
and breakthroughs verbally, while Shawn wrote them on a pad
(the image below is one such scene).
(Yup, André had discovered a new comic �lter on his phone, so every picture he took was like this.)
Unlike other promotions, this one was really an expression of the
work we were doing for a client. We put our takeaways into an
email each evening, which André sent to everyone on the interest
segment.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 34 --

The insight is that we were learning about our own creative
process as we did the work for our client. Each email became a
valuable gift to our audience as we shared freely. Emails never felt
like promotions. We journaled a story that was revealing itself to
us, which was immensely valuable in and of itself (we included
those images in the emails, which people seemed to love, as they
added color and texture to the narrative).
Because André was scheduled to �y back to Europe on Friday 29th
(Black Friday), we created the offer page the night before
(Thanksgiving), just before going out for dinner.
The promotion ended up attracting just shy of 200 customers (at
$225), which was great. It paid for the trip (and then some) and
helped us validate an idea. ITA will turn into a full course. All our
customers from this promotion get all future course updates for
life. Win-win.
▪ Monday (Nov 25th): NYC Trip Insights (Day 1)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 34 --

▪ Tuesday (Nov 26th): NYC Trip Insights (Day 2)
▪ Wednesday (Nov 27th): NYC Trip Insights (Day 3)
▪ Friday (Nov 29th): NYC Trip (Black Friday)
▪ Sunday (Dec 1): Ideas to Assets Masterclass
▪ Monday (Dec 2): Final Day! (Cyber Monday)
If you have questions about this speci�c PLS, let us know in the
comments.
Case Study 2: The Traf�c Engine (April 1-7,
2020)
Thesis/theme (controlling idea): The Tra�c Engine existed only in concept
prior to the April 1 — 7 promotion. The goal was to share actionable
principles for creating stable, predictable, and scaleable sources of paid
tra�c for online businesses from Shawn’s twenty-one years running a
digital-only agency. Each day’s comments and email feedback further
re�ned the direction of our email content (and the course content) to
ensure both matched the audience’s needs.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 34 --

Above is a glimpse of the text conversations between us. What
should become evident is that our process is very organic. We
come up with our controlling idea, then everything we write falls
within that.
Because Shawn is the paid traf�c expert, he led each email (you’ll
see this �ow revealed when you read the campaign emails). He
would write, then email it to André. In the morning (we’re six
hours apart), André would read what he wrote, then allow it to
inform whatever expressed itself for him. André could never plan
what to write because he didn’t know what Shawn would write.
This �ow works very well.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 34 --

www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 34 --

www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 34 --

▪ Tue (Mar 24, 2020) — TTE: American Airlines (Email 1)
▪ Wed (Mar 25, 2020) — TTE: Where do I start? (Email 2)
▪ Thu (Mar 26, 2020) — TTE: Structure + Tip (Email 3)
▪ Fri (Mar 27, 2020) — TTE: Pricing Framework (Email 4)
▪ Mon (Mar 30, 2020) — TTE: Ladybug Marketing Effect
(Email 5)
▪ Tue (Mar 31, 2020) — TTE: Freeze Frame (Email 6)
▪ Wed (Apr 1, 2020) — CART OPEN
▪ Fri (Apr 3, 2020) — TTE: Testing Framework (Email 7)
▪ Sun (Apr 5, 2020) — TTE: Iterate to Success (Email 8)
▪ Mon (Apr 6, 2020) — TTE: Just One Person (Email 9)
▪ Tue (Apr 7, 2020) — TTE: Resources (Email 10)
For TTE we built the offer (product) page from the get-go, which
was initially structured like this:
�. Intro (where Shawn was introduced)
�. Paid Traf�c Manifesto
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 34 --

�. Email Archives
�. Comments (we use Commento)
As the launch progressed, we added a Q&A section and Course
Outline. But the bulk of the interactions happened through the
email campaign (which we published directly to the website right
after sending each email) and website comments.
In the end, we had over 20,000 words in the comments and over
15,000 words in the emails. That’s excluding the manifesto and
other content on the offer page. This all evolved, bit by bit, email
by email, as the three-week campaign played out.
Email was the driver of everything. All emails pointed back to the
comments where interactions happened. Our interactions with
prospects informed ideas for emails, and the �ywheel turned.
The insight here is that we used comments as a mechanism to
have open discussions with our audience, which helped inform
the emails we jointly wrote.
We encourage people to ‘hit reply’ to emails to ask questions and
share insights for other promotions. Emails also aren’t archived
directly to the site each day as we did here, which is something
we’ll do after a promotion. Neither approach is objectively better.
We pick the approach that feels right and go with it. We can
always iterate along the way.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 34 --

Our promo segment was 1819 people. During the promotion, 37
people “muted” themselves from the segment pool. TTE did a little
over 340 core orders ($375) — approximately 19% of the segment
converted — with around 25% taking the client services add-on
(extra $225).
If you have questions about this speci�c PLS, let us know in the
comments.
Case Study 3: SOI & ARM (June 1-8, 2020)
Thesis/theme (controlling idea): This was a little more complex. Our idea
was to unpack the importance of doing the upfront work to create a better
prospect (SOI), and then build relationships via email to create customers,
even before a transaction (ARM). Then wrap this all into a worldbuilding
narrative.
The process for SOI and ARM was a reversal of TTE, but with a
twist. Instead of our contribution being separate, we created one
narrative voice.
In the book The Undoing Project, Michael Lewis (author) explored
the power of collaboration between Amos Tversky and Daniel
Kahneman. Their work introduced the notion of cognitive biases
and illusions, and their contributions to behavioral economics and
decision-making have been called “breathtakingly important.”
That’s how Michael Lewis described their collaboration. The big
insight was that the work produced through their collaboration
www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 34 --

was not only completely different than either of them had done
on his own, but orders of magnitude better. In many ways it’s
harder to write as one narrative voice, but the result was worth
the effort.
This time André (mostly) wrote the lead, bounced over to Shawn
(we use Ulysses pointing at a shared Dropbox folder), who would
overlay his contribution and make edits. André would do the �nal
once over, send out as a broadcast, then publish the web-version.
▪ Tue (May 26, 2020) — I’m so glad I found you!
▪ Wed (May 27, 2020) — Worldbuilding (Part 1)
▪ Thu (May 28, 2020) — Worldbuilding (Part 2)
▪ Fri (May 29, 2020) — Dudes. Take my money!
▪ Sun (May 31, 2020) — Heat (The Movie)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 34 --

▪ Mon (June 1, 2020) — Enrollment is Open
▪ Tue (June 2, 2020) — Push vs. Pull
▪ Wed (June 3, 2020) — Two Realities, One Prospect, & The
Secret Garden
▪ Thu (June 4, 2020) — Is Big Bad?
▪ Fri (June 5, 2020) — Relationship Marketing & Making
Customers
▪ Sun (June 7, 2020) — Q&A Email (both SOI & ARM)
▪ Mon (June 8, 2020) — Final Email (w/ Gift)
We opened both SOI and ARM for our summer enrollment and
rolled them up into one giant campaign. We thought this would
be a valuable experience for anyone considering either (or both)
because they would get to see (and experience) how the two
systems synergistically working together.
The challenge for us was where do we put the email archive and
table of contents; on the SOI page or ARM page? In the end, we
decided to put it on our product page, which you can see here:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 34 --

We included Amazon Polly audio for all our emails to help with
consumption.
Our promo segment was 1214 people. During the promotion, 28
people “muted” themselves from the segment pool. SOI + ARM
www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 34 --

did around 280 core orders ($495) — approximately 22% of the
segment converted — with around 100 taking the swipe �le add-
on (extra $97).
If you have questions about this speci�c PLS, let us know in the
comments.
Shawn’s perspective:
Let me begin by reinforcing something André mentioned
earlier — our process is organic and unique to us. The
outcomes of that process (the emails you saw above), also are
unique. They occurred within different contexts and that
matters too.
ITA was the �rst time we had ever collaborated together (and
the only time I had ever worked with a creative partner). TTE
happened at the beginning of the COVID-19 lockdown in the
U.S. The June 2020 SOI + ARM promotion was weeks after
André and I had become business partners.
Each context in�uenced our writing. And each time we wrote
together, we explored new ideas and nuances. We re�ned our
individual voices, and we developed a voice that’s ours
together (yet neither of us individually).
Your experience will be similar. Your writing will evolve. Your
voice will too.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 19 of 34 --

A word of warning — don’t try to reverse-engineer the
campaigns we created. Why? Because they weren’t ever
engineered in the �rst place! If you look for clues in the
overall structure of what we did, please know that those
campaigns were written from the ground up, not the top
down.
André and I are both writers, both deep thinkers, and we each
have decades of experience in our �eld. We also have
exceptionally similar values, and we genuinely like and
respect each other.
Our emails re�ect what emerges when all of those factors are
mixed together. I can’t even begin to express how much fun it
is to write with André. It’s hard — of course — but the quality
of the experience is beyond words. That’s a signi�cant factor
too.
The emails you create should re�ect all of your individual
quirks and nuances. Whatever makes you you should show up
in your writing. Give yourself permission to be fully you,
whatever that means. Don’t worry about the formalities of
‘copywriting’ or any of that nonsense. Just write — from your
head and your heart.
Looking back, it’s clear to me that I was following a set of
deeply internalized guidelines. I’ll share those here in case
they’re valuable to you as well.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 20 of 34 --

This isn’t a sequential checklist, or anything I have
formalized. It’s best described as an internal compass that
guides my preparation and my writing with a series of
questions.
Question #1 — is what I want to write interesting to me and
to the reader? Is there something about it that’s counter-
intuitive, curious, informative, or enjoyable?
Question #2 — is what I want to write relevant to the person
who will be reading it? Does it answer a question s/he has? Is
it something that s/he cares about?
Question #3 — is what I want to write valuable to the reader?
Can s/he do something new, different, or better because of it?
Can I improve his life in some small, yet meaningful way in
exchange for his attention?
Question #4 — is what I want to write congruent with, and
representative of, what I believe and teach? Am I walking my
talk in what I’m writing and how I’m writing it?
Question #5 — am I writing to the reader like s/he is already
a customer, regardless if money has (or ever does) change
hands? Am I treating her like a good friend?
I’m not suggesting that you use these same questions (please
feel free to borrow any that resonate with you). They’re
www.getwsodo.com

-- 21 of 34 --

representative of how I want to show up in the world and
they’re meaningful to me.
One of my deeply held beliefs is that leading with empathy
and value for others will create value for me as a by-product.
André and I never coerce, persuade, or ‘sell’ in our promotions
— we teach, demonstrate, and model because that’s who we
are.
It’s important to both of us that if you were to peel back the
curtain and really see what we do, day to day, it would look
exactly like we describe and teach.
A few �nal thoughts — be kind to yourself. It’s so easy to look
at examples, feel demoralized, and think to yourself ‘I could
never do that’. Trust me, you can. Maybe not in the same way
or the same voice, but in your own way, and your own voice
you absolutely can do it.
But the only way to �nd out is to put your �ngers on the
keyword and start typing. Externalize your thoughts, polish
them so they sound right when you read them to yourself,
and send them out into the world.
When they come back with feedback, listen deeply, then sit
down and write again. That’s the mountain we’re all climbing.
Welcome to the club.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 22 of 34 --

Case Study 4: The Durable Business (Black
Friday, 2020)
Thesis/theme (controlling idea): Our idea was to draw attention to
the reality that �nancial success is an emergent property of
effectively meeting the needs and addressing the fears of an
audience that values the bene�ts you’re able to create speci�cally
for them.
We had an idea to rebrand a course André had created through
this lens but never “of�cially” released, called Lean Business for
Creators. LBC already had nearly 700 customers from his original
2018 Black Friday early adopter enrollment (and a subsequent
enrollment). Nearly 700 customers were a strong signal that our
audience resonated with the idea, so we wanted to double down
and �nally create this course.
We framed our idea in our Nov 6th VNL, which queued up an
interview with our dear friend, Jonathan Boyd. This gave us all the
ingredients for a narrative we felt would be valuable to everyone.
We used our VNL as the vehicle for our bridge emails on Nov 6th
and 20th. Nov 23rd through Cyber Monday (30th) went to this
segment.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 23 of 34 --

You can access the chronology of the email promotion here.
Note: When we renamed AutoResponder Madness to Art of Email,
all ARM customers received AoE for free. We did the same here,
giving free access to The Durable Business to all 700 LBC
customers.
Our segment was 1098 people, of those, 11 muted themselves from
the segment pool, with just shy of 190 new customers ($395).
If you have questions about this speci�c PLS, let us know in the
comments.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 24 of 34 --

The Setup
Making a PLS happen is functionally very easy.
Not all these steps are required, and there are some decisions
you’ll need to make, either from promotion to promotion, or
globally.
The �rst decision, which has implications, is do you continue to
send promo emails to people who have purchased?
Typically, the �rst or second largest sales day will be the opening
day, with the �nal day generating as many sales, if not a little
more. Let’s look at the two options — with a third as a
consideration.
Option 1 — when someone becomes a customer, they are removed
from the live promotion. This is probably your default play to
avoid confusion.
Option 2 — customers continue to receive the promotion after
purchase, which can seem jarring unless you’re transparent (in
advance) why you’re doing this.
As observation we made years ago, is that some (or many) people
would RESIST purchasing, because they WANTED to receive an
entire promotion. This seemed odd, until we dug deeper. Turns
out, those people loved our AoE-inspired PLSs so much, they
www.getwsodo.com

-- 25 of 34 --

wanted to experience the entire promotion.
Think about that for a moment — people who already purchased
WANTED to keep receiving emails.
To address this, we’ve mentioned early on that everyone (including
new customers) will continue to receive a promotion all the way
through, and if they wanted out, they only need to click the
“mute” link.
We also do something unique with our promotions that started
with the April 2020 release of The Traf�c Engine.
There was a lot of excitement during the TTE promotion and we
were concerned that some people might buy to avoid feeling left
out when enrollment closed.
To prevent that, we created an eight-week mini-marketing MBA
course and gave that way for free on the day enrollment closed.
That was our way of saying thanks to our audience for their
attention while discouraging people from buying who shouldn’t.
We liked that idea so much that we’ve given something away for
free in every PLS since.
At �rst glance, giving something away at the end of a promotion
seems counter-productive because it very likely decreases �nal-
day conversions. However, when we realize we’re optimizing for
the long-term health of our business by over-investing in
www.getwsodo.com

-- 26 of 34 --

relationships with our audience, the short-term advantage of a
few more sales is far outweighed by the long-term advantages
from treating everyone the way we’d like to be treated.
If you experiment with this idea, we recommend that you don’t
mention in your Bridge Emails that you’ll be giving something
away in your PLS. (We’ve done that and we suspect that we
encouraged people interested in the free course who were not
otherwise interested in the promotion.)
Depending on the PLS, we may choose option 1 or 2. Or, if we give
away a free give, we’ll choose Option 3 — that’s the same as
Option 1, but everyone receives the “free gift” email, which is
communicated to them at the start of the promo.
In simpler terms, this is what each of the options looks like.
Option 1 (PLS with no free gift): customers are removed from the
PLS after purchase.
Option 2 (PLS with no free gift): customers continue to receive
the PLS emails after purchase (unless they mute the promotion).
Option 3 (PLS with free gift): customers are removed from the
PLS after purchase but they receive the free gift email.
With that all say, let’s look at the ingredients required:
▪ Link Trigger (to segment hand raisers from your bridge
www.getwsodo.com

-- 27 of 34 --

emails)
▪ (tag) Interested - NAME - META (see SPP, Part 2 for suggested
taxonomy for tagging)
▪ (tag) Mute - NAME - META
▪ Create Sequence (for E0)
▪ Broadcasts (for the promotion schedule)
▪ Keep updating E0 Sequence with archived emails
▪ World Building (expand your world)
E0 Sequence
The E0 (zero) sequence is a single email sequence that is sent to
everyone who segments themselves into a PLS anytime AFTER a
promotion has started.
Bridge emails, whether solo affairs, or within a VNL, live in
inboxes way beyond the time-limited constraints of a PLS, so we
need a way to account for these late comers to the party.
We put an E0 live right after the �rst broadcast email. Before then,
hand raisers are only “tagged” as interested, example:
▪ 	Interested - AoE - Oct 2021
▪ 	Interested - AoE Live Workshop - Oct 2021
Once both Interested and Mute tags are created, we create a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 28 of 34 --

broadcast segment. Also note, if we wanted to suppress all new
customers from the broadcast segment, we would also add our
customer tag (example: Purchased - MBW to the NONE list, below):
Cohort 2 enrollment for Momentum Building Workshop.
With these prerequisites in place — and after the �rst broadcast
has been sent — we put the E0 Sequence live:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 29 of 34 --

Caption: E0 Sequence for Oct 2021 AoE enrollment.
Note: Emails in the red box are added each day as the PLS
progresses. When you �rst put this live, it’ll only contain a link to
the �rst broadcast email.
All broadcast emails in ConvertKit automatically get a web-
archive version. Click into any broadcast email. Then on the right,
you’ll see a button that says, Share.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 30 of 34 --

Clicking on it will reveal the archived web-version. Here’s the
archive for the email above.
For our AoE / Workshop enrollment (Oct 2021), we had 44 people
come in late to the joint promo (of which 7 purchased — or 16%),
and 25 to the ARM-only promo (12 of which purchased — or 48%).
We share these stats to reinforce why we do this. We work hard to
make this a delightful experience for everyone, even those people
who show up late.
World Building
Over and above using the built-in archived email feature of
ConvertKit, we create full web-versions of many of our promo
emails (catalog example here, and product-speci�c example here,
where we link to emails directly from a product page).
More broadly, we repurpose many of our VNL emails to our
www.getwsodo.com

-- 31 of 34 --

website. We do this for three reasons.
Reason 1 — a Product Launch Series does something special —
even magical — to people engaged in a narrative arc, interacting
with the content, and greedily wanting more (a decision void of
any coerced pressure). It has an invisible “tugging” and pulling
effect in the minds of our best prospects.
That’s an effect we don’t want to waste.
So, by repurposing some of these emails (and VNL emails), we
help future customers �nd new “doorways” into different parts of
our world. In turn, discovering new perspectives around our
thinking and our products, producing long-term paradigm-
shifting bene�ts.
We in�uence when we help someone come to conclusions on their
own. We do this by creating insights and hidden “Easter Eggs” to
�nd. Thus, we create an environment where in�uence is earned,
and relationships can �ourish.
This is based on a long-term agreement where a prospect is lead
to a conclusion on their own that feels right for them. In one
word: resonance.
Reason 2 — implicit in the above reason, we publish emails to our
site to expand our world building (a concept from SOI, and
explained here and within AoE here).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 32 of 34 --

There are two parts to our ‘world’ — the outer world and the inner
world. The outer world is everything publicly accessible on our
website (stories, articles, blog posts series, manifestos, presells,
free email series, etc.). The inner world is the content behind the
“wall”; our courses within the Academy (including workshops,
masterminds, coaching, etc.), and the emails we send to people on
our email list.
One of our Strategic Upstream Decisions is that people become
customers way before money has ever changed hands. To this
end, we get a constant stream of people reaching out to us after
having found our content and consumed every word of it, then
expressing how excited they are for when we open next.
Think about how powerful that is.
This can only happen when we make it possible for prospects to
discover parts of our world on their own, to experience the
insights we’ve purposefully included in every email, and for the
synergistic effect of all the emails coming together to create an
emergent affect in the mind of a prospect.
Better prospects are created — as we unpacked in our SOI
manifesto — which is why we want our best content to be found
by future customers.
Reason 3 — �nally, expanding our outer world is also perfect
‘spider food.’ SEO (search engine optimization). We don’t write
www.getwsodo.com

-- 33 of 34 --

SEO optimized emails. That’s not a focus or even a consideration.
But when we publish our emails as website content, we can apply
basic SEO 101. Over time, this works to attract future customers
that are pulled towards us.
Win-win-win.
Please post your comments, questions, and insights below.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 34 of 34 --
